sets.title: Supplemental Regulations, Source Water, and Situational Practice
sets.quiz_page_title: Supplemental Regulations, Source Water, and Situational Practice - Quiz Page
sets.answer_key_page_title: Supplemental Regulations, Source Water, and Situational Practice - Answer Key

questions.prompt: Which federal law authorizes EPA to set national health-based drinking water standards for public water systems?
questions.explanation: The SDWA is the main federal drinking-water law authorizing EPA drinking-water standards for public water systems.

questions.prompt: Which federal regulation part contains National Primary Drinking Water Regulations for public water systems?
questions.explanation: 40 CFR Part 141 contains the National Primary Drinking Water Regulations.

questions.prompt: Which federal regulation part is most associated with state primacy and implementation of the drinking-water program?
questions.explanation: 40 CFR Part 142 addresses implementation, primacy, and related state program requirements.

questions.prompt: Which federal regulation part covers National Secondary Drinking Water Regulations?
questions.explanation: 40 CFR Part 143 is the secondary drinking-water regulation part.

questions.prompt: For California drinking-water MCLs and routine drinking-water monitoring requirements, which source should an operator expect to use most often?

choices.B: Clean Water Act NPDES permit only

questions.explanation: California drinking-water standards and monitoring are primarily in CCR Title 22.
questions.source_document: CCR Title 22 / State Water Board lawbook

questions.prompt: For current California cross-connection control program requirements, what should operators treat as the current controlling source?
questions.explanation: California replaced prior Title 17 cross-connection regulations with the Cross-Connection Control Policy Handbook.

questions.prompt: California T1 through T5 and D1 through D5 operator certification requirements are primarily associated with which California regulation area?

choices.C: AWIA emergency response plan rules only

questions.explanation: California operator certification requirements are in the Title 22 operator certification framework.
questions.source_document: CCR Title 22 operator certification / State Water Board DWOCP

questions.prompt: Which rule family is most directly tied to routine bacteriological monitoring, total coliform, E. coli findings, assessments, and corrective action?
questions.explanation: The Revised Total Coliform Rule covers the total coliform/E. coli bacteriological framework and assessment response.

questions.prompt: Which rule family is most associated with filtration, turbidity performance, CT, Giardia, viruses, and Cryptosporidium?
questions.explanation: Surface-water treatment rules address filtration, disinfection, turbidity, CT, and microbial inactivation/removal targets.
questions.source_document: Surface Water Treatment Rule family / 40 CFR Part 141

questions.prompt: Which rule family is most associated with TTHM and HAA5 compliance?

choices.A: Stage 1 and Stage 2 Disinfectants and Disinfection Byproducts Rules

questions.explanation: The DBPR rule family covers disinfectants and disinfection byproducts such as TTHM and HAA5.

questions.prompt: Lead and copper compliance is most associated with which regulatory concept?

choices.B: Action level and corrosion control framework

questions.explanation: Lead and copper compliance uses action levels and corrosion-control related requirements rather than a simple finished-water MCL model.
questions.source_document: Lead and Copper Rule / LCRI transition context

questions.prompt: A confirmed E. coli positive routine sample is reported. What is the best operator response?

choices.A: Wait for the annual CCR cycle before doing anything
choices.B: Follow the bacteriological rule response plan, notify required contacts, and coordinate repeat/corrective action requirements
choices.D: Ignore it if chlorine residual is usually present

questions.explanation: A confirmed E. coli issue requires rule-driven response, notification/coordination, and corrective action handling.

questions.prompt: A source-water blending calculation is usually based on which principle?

choices.A: The highest source concentration always becomes the blended concentration
choices.C: The lowest source concentration always becomes the blended concentration
choices.D: Only tank volume matters; source flow does not

questions.explanation: Blended water quality is estimated with a flow-weighted concentration from each contributing source.

questions.prompt: Source A is 2 MGD at 8 mg/L nitrate and Source B is 1 MGD at 2 mg/L nitrate. Approximate blended nitrate is:
A high-arsenic well is 0.5 MGD at 12 ug/L and a low-arsenic source is 1.5 MGD at 4 ug/L. Approximate blended arsenic is:
Before changing source-water blending ratios for compliance, what should a chief operator do first?

choices.A: Change valves and wait for complaints
choices.B: Verify permit/operational limits, calculate expected quality, coordinate required monitoring, and document the change
choices.C: Assume blending always removes all contaminants

questions.explanation: Source blending changes can affect compliance and distribution quality, so they should be calculated, documented, and coordinated with required monitoring/permits.

questions.prompt: A new source blend may lower nitrate but increase TDS and customer taste complaints. What is the best operator-level concern?

choices.B: Both primary compliance and secondary/aesthetic water quality impacts should be evaluated
choices.C: Taste complaints prove the water is unsafe
choices.D: Secondary standards override all primary standards

questions.explanation: Blending decisions should consider enforceable health standards and secondary/aesthetic impacts that affect distribution quality and customers.
questions.source_document: 40 CFR 141/143 and Title 22 secondary standards context

questions.prompt: A wildfire causes loss of power, pressure fluctuations, and possible source-water ash impacts. What is the best T5-level first move?

choices.A: Wait until all lab results are complete before communicating internally
choices.B: Activate the emergency response plan, stabilize pressure/treatment, coordinate notifications, and begin required monitoring

questions.explanation: Emergency response should stabilize operations, coordinate communication, and start appropriate monitoring and notifications.

questions.prompt: A monitoring result is above an MCL. Which action is most appropriate?

choices.A: Erase the sample from the log if a resample might pass
choices.B: Follow the applicable confirmation, reporting, public-notice, and corrective-action requirements
choices.C: Only tell customers if they ask
choices.D: Wait until next year's CCR before reviewing the result

questions.explanation: MCL exceedances trigger rule-specific confirmation, reporting, public notification, and corrective-action requirements.
questions.source_document: 40 CFR Part 141 / Title 22 public notification and monitoring

questions.prompt: A customer asks why the system issued a Tier 1 public notice. What is the best general explanation?

choices.A: Tier 1 is for situations with immediate potential health concern requiring rapid public notice
choices.B: Tier 1 is only an annual education notice
choices.C: Tier 1 is only for billing errors
choices.D: Tier 1 means no operational response is needed

questions.explanation: Tier 1 public notice is used for the most urgent drinking-water situations with immediate potential health concern.

questions.prompt: A compliance sample was analyzed by a lab that is not properly accredited for the method. What is the best concern?

choices.A: The result may not be acceptable for compliance reporting
choices.B: The result is automatically better than an accredited result
choices.D: The operator can certify the lab result personally

questions.explanation: Compliance samples generally need proper approved methods and accredited laboratory handling for regulatory acceptance.

questions.prompt: Which law is more associated with wastewater discharges, NPDES permits, and source-water protection context than finished drinking-water MCLs?
questions.explanation: The Clean Water Act is mainly tied to water pollution control and discharges, though it affects source-water context.
questions.source_document: Clean Water Act / source-water protection context

questions.prompt: Which California law is most associated with broad water-quality control, basin planning, and discharge/source-water context?
questions.explanation: Porter-Cologne is California's broad water-quality control law, more source/discharge oriented than operator MCL tables.

questions.prompt: Which federal rule requires annual water quality reporting to consumers?
questions.explanation: The Consumer Confidence Report Rule is the annual customer water-quality report framework.

questions.prompt: Which rule area is most associated with gross alpha, uranium, radium, and beta/photon emitters?
questions.explanation: The radionuclides rule area covers gross alpha, uranium, radium, and beta/photon emitters.

questions.prompt: PFAS monitoring and MCL implementation is most associated with which area?

choices.A: The federal PFAS National Primary Drinking Water Regulation and state implementation requirements
choices.B: Only the old Title 17 cross-connection articles

questions.explanation: PFAS requirements are part of the emerging/current drinking-water regulatory program and are implemented through federal and state compliance processes.

questions.prompt: A filter effluent turbidity trend rises after a source blend change. What should the operator check first?

choices.B: Raw water quality, coagulant dose/jar testing, filter performance, and monitoring requirements

questions.explanation: A source blend can change treatment demand, so raw water quality and process-control checks are appropriate.
questions.source_document: Treatment process control / source blending practice

questions.prompt: A source blend lowers turbidity but increases chlorine demand. What should the operator evaluate?

choices.A: Whether disinfectant dose, contact time, residual, and DBP risk remain acceptable
choices.B: Only the color of the pump station
choices.C: Nothing, because lower turbidity always solves every issue

questions.explanation: Blending can shift disinfection demand and DBP formation potential, so dose, residual, contact time, and DBP risk should be reviewed.

questions.prompt: A chlorine feed pump loses prime during peak demand. What is the best operator response?

choices.A: Document it at the end of the month only
choices.B: Restore/backup feed safely, verify residuals, assess affected water, notify supervision, and follow SOP/reporting triggers
choices.D: Assume contact time alone is always enough

questions.explanation: A disinfectant feed failure requires safe restoration, verification of residual and affected water, and escalation under SOP/regulatory triggers.

questions.prompt: A flow of 1,496 gpm is approximately:
questions.explanation: 1,496 gpm x 1,440 min/day = 2,154,240 gal/day, or about 2.15 MGD.
